narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 42.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students, focusing on factors like early academic rankings, test scores, and backgrounds that correlate with bar success. The model's call hinged most heavily on three key elements of your profile: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3 (below the average 5.53), and your LSAT score of 30 (under the typical 36.70). Your strong undergrad GPA was the biggest positive force, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, as it signals the kind of consistent performance that often leads to success. In contrast, your lower Year 3 decile ranking had a notable pull in the opposite direction, nudging the model toward anticipating a failure because students in lower deciles like yours have historically struggled more on the bar. Your LSAT score reinforced that concern, contributing toward the failure prediction since scores in the 30 range tend to align with tougher bar outcomes compared to higher performers. While your middle family income bracket and full-time student status are common and didn't sway things much, these academic markers painted a picture riskier than the profiles of students who typically get approved. Many admitted applicants have stronger mid-to-late school rankings and LSATs that offset any weaker spots, placing them firmly in the "likely to pass" zone. Your situation, though promising in undergrad performance, fell short in those later indicators relative to peers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted study or additional coursework, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience as a Black male navigating these challenges. Reapplying with those improvements could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
